anchor_paper.why: Best category-A anchor for the beat: it consolidates the recursive-reuse collapse argument at high visibility, gives the cleanest statement of tail-loss under indiscriminate synthetic retraining, and sets up the needed scope-limiting contrast with later mitigation papers.

spine.role_in_narrative: Opens the beat by establishing the core hazard: recursive training on model-generated outputs can erase rare modes and induce collapse.
spine.transition_to_next: Nature 2024 takes this initial warning and turns it into a more general, prominent statement: when reuse is indiscriminate, recursive synthetic training drives irreversible tail loss.

spine.role_in_narrative: Anchor paper that crystallizes collapse risk as a serious motivation for worrying about self-reinforcing synthetic data loops.
spine.transition_to_next: At the same time, this line of work mostly demonstrates risk under indiscriminate recursive reuse, not that all synthetic data is harmful; the next step is to mark that boundary explicitly.

spine.role_in_narrative: Provides the required scope-limiting turn: self-consuming loops can be stabilized under corrective conditions, so collapse is contingent rather than universal.
spine.transition_to_next: Once collapse is treated as a conditional risk rather than a universal law, the motivating question shifts to the information environment: are real domains accumulating enough synthetic material for those risky feedback conditions to matter?

spine.role_in_narrative: First bridge into contamination pressure, using a concrete domain-specific measurement of LLM-written or LLM-modified scientific text.
spine.transition_to_next: But domain-specific uptake in science is not yet proof of uniform web-scale contamination, so the next literature asks whether retrieval environments show stronger downstream exposure effects.

spine.role_in_narrative: Makes the contamination-pressure bridge more concrete by showing that retrieval systems can become overexposed to synthetic content even before complete pool saturation.
spine.transition_to_next: If information environments are plausibly under contamination pressure, an obvious response is to detect and filter synthetic outputs; the detector literature, however, shows that this backstop is brittle under adaptation.

spine.role_in_narrative: Closes the beat by showing the limits of reactive filtering: text detectors can be defeated by paraphrase, even if retrieval-based defenses remain partially helpful.

supporting.role: Structural follow-on theory that formalizes collapse as distributional sharpening and diversity loss, reinforcing the tail-collapse mechanism.
Applied domain example showing collapse-like erosion of rare pathological findings and diagnostic reliability in synthetic medical data.
Formal counterexample showing that curated self-consuming loops can avoid collapse and even optimize toward human preferences.
Empirical counterexample in diffusion models: synthetic data can be useful when introduced through carefully designed negative-guidance mechanisms.
Additional mitigation-oriented evidence that anti-collapse training designs can improve generation rather than degrade it.
Broader but noisier web snapshot suggesting AI assistance is widespread on new pages, while also showing that fully AI-only pages remain a minority.
Volume-based estimate reinforcing growth in AI-authored web text, but also usefully tempering the claim by noting ranked articles remain mostly human-written.
Domain-specific prevalence evidence for synthetic political imagery, supporting the idea of concentrated contamination pressure rather than universal saturation.
Educational-domain prevalence study showing that synthetic uptake appears in bounded text environments beyond the web-at-large.
News-media prevalence study from one language ecosystem; useful as supporting evidence precisely because it remains domain- and locale-specific.
Important partial defense: watermarking can enable reliable detection under cooperative generation settings, preventing an overclaim that detection simply cannot work.
Survey-level synthesis showing that detectability varies by generator, domain, and method, supporting a fragility claim rather than an impossibility claim.

paragraph_outline.topic: Recursive synthetic reuse creates a real collapse risk, but the literature itself already shows that the strongest claims concern indiscriminate reuse rather than all synthetic-data regimes.
paragraph_outline.opening_sentence: The literature first establishes a genuine recursive-reuse hazard—especially loss of rare structure and tail diversity—while also making clear that this is not a blanket indictment of all synthetic data.

paragraph_outline.topic: After that scope limit, the bridge to contamination is empirical and still incomplete: several studies show synthetic uptake in bounded information environments, and retrieval work suggests these pressures can amplify exposure before total saturation.
paragraph_outline.opening_sentence: With collapse reframed as a contingent risk, the key motivating question becomes whether real information environments are accumulating enough synthetic material for those conditions to arise in practice.

paragraph_outline.topic: Reactive filtering is only a partial safeguard: prevalence studies increasingly rely on detectors, yet detector-focused work shows easy evasion, uneven performance, and only conditional defenses.
paragraph_outline.opening_sentence: A final motivation is that even if contamination pressure is only partially mapped, the obvious remedy—detect and filter synthetic content—looks fragile once generators adapt and detection moves across domains.

writing_notes: Keep the prose explicitly motivational: this beat raises risk and pressure conditions but is not the direct evidence base for the later primary post-training claim. Preserve the three-step chain: recursive reuse risk, partial contamination-pressure evidence, then detector fragility. After the Nature paper, insert an explicit sentence such as: 'Importantly, this literature mainly establishes failure under indiscriminate recursive reuse, not universal synthetic-data failure.' Use Self-Correcting Self-Consuming Loops and Curated Data as the in-set counterevidence, and note that an additional mixed real+synthetic non-collapse pathway (the pi^2/6 line) exists but is not represented in the supplied candidate set, so it should be acknowledged in prose as external counterevidence rather than omitted. When discussing category-B papers, say contamination is demonstrated in specific environments—science papers, new webpages, political images, MOOCs, selected news ecosystems, retrieval pools—not uniformly proven at web scale. When discussing category-C papers, avoid saying detection is impossible; say instead that detection is context-dependent, often evadable under paraphrase or distribution shift, and partly supplemented by watermarking or retrieval-based defenses.

anchor_paper.why: It is the clearest anchor for this beat because it turns abstract concerns about drift into a concrete longitudinal measurement program: a large, curated, time-spanning web-domain dataset that shows measurable change without overstating those changes as web-wide contamination.

spine.role_in_narrative: Establishes the measurement problem: corpus-level drift is often observed through proxy statistics, but those statistics vary in robustness to corpus size and content, so any drift signal must be treated as indirect.
spine.transition_to_next: Because proxy metrics alone cannot identify where web drift is happening, the narrative moves to longitudinal datasets that observe concrete changes within specific web domains over time.

spine.role_in_narrative: Provides the first strong longitudinal observation: a million-document privacy-policy corpus showing that at least one major web genre changes measurably across decades and in response to regulation.
spine.transition_to_next: Having established a curated longitudinal corpus, the next paper deepens the claim by analyzing how the content of that same web genre changed from 1996 to 2021.

spine.role_in_narrative: Extends the privacy-policy line from collection to substantive content drift, showing measurable longitudinal shifts while still remaining domain-specific rather than web-wide.
spine.transition_to_next: These domain-level observations motivate a broader question for LLM data: how crawl builders detect, document, and filter changing web content in practice.

spine.role_in_narrative: Shifts from observing web change to documenting crawl artifacts, showing that a major pretraining corpus already contains unexpected content, benchmark leakage, and filtering distortions.
spine.transition_to_next: If large crawls contain such artifacts, the next step is not to infer collapse, but to ask whether stronger filtering and deduplication can still make web data useful.

spine.role_in_narrative: Supplies required counterevidence: filtered, deduplicated web-only data can outperform curated corpora, so measurable crawl issues do not by themselves imply that web pretraining data has broadly degraded.
spine.transition_to_next: FineWeb then extends this filtration-and-scaling story, testing whether careful curation over many Common Crawl snapshots preserves or improves training value.

spine.role_in_narrative: Closes the chain with stronger counterevidence from a larger, more recent web dataset: extensive filtering across many crawl snapshots still yields strong models, which narrows the conclusion to partial, indirect measurability of drift rather than demonstrated web-wide contamination.
spine.transition_to_next: The beat should therefore end on a scope boundary: this corpus contains no post-2022 web-scale contamination audit, so the literature supports rising risk and partial observability, not decisive proof of web-wide failure.

supporting.role: Shows that topic-model-based drift summaries depend on hyperparameters and regularization choices, reinforcing that proxy measurements are method-sensitive.
Adds a second topic-model proxy paper, cited from the prior study, to emphasize that even refined entropy-based topic signals remain indirect summaries of corpus change.
Motivates why lexical, syntactic, and semantic diversity are plausible warning signals under synthetic recursion, while not measuring open-web drift directly.
Provides a compact statistical link between entropy and type-token ratio, useful for framing why common diversity proxies often move together.
Broadens the longitudinal picture by showing macro-level shifts in what kinds of sites dominate the web over 25+ years.
Adds evidence that online attention concentrates around dominant firms, suggesting structural drift in where web text and user activity accumulate.
Provides a platform-specific contemporary signal that AI-generated content is visibly entering a major knowledge site, but only at a local platform scale.
Adds a neighboring post-2023 web-change pressure—consent and access restrictions—that affects crawl composition, while remaining distinct from a contamination audit.

paragraph_outline.topic: Measurement proxies can signal distributional change, but they are indirect and method-sensitive.
paragraph_outline.opening_sentence: The most accessible evidence for web drift comes from proxy statistics such as topic structure, entropy, and lexical diversity, but this literature mainly shows that such measurements are possible and fragile, not that web-wide contamination has been demonstrated.

paragraph_outline.topic: Longitudinal web studies show measurable change in specific domains and platforms, not a full web-scale contamination audit.
paragraph_outline.opening_sentence: Moving from abstract proxies to observed web history, longitudinal studies make clear that some web domains and platforms drift substantially over time, but the evidence remains segmented by genre, site class, or platform rather than truly web-wide.

paragraph_outline.topic: Crawl-curation papers document risk signals but also provide counterevidence that filtered web data remains highly effective.
paragraph_outline.opening_sentence: At the corpus-construction level, the literature documents benchmark leakage, unexpected content, and shifting consent conditions in web crawls, yet it also shows that aggressive filtering and deduplication can still produce very strong pretraining data—and no post-2022 web-scale contamination audit exists in this corpus.

writing_notes: Keep the tone explicitly motivational. The argument should be: drift is partly measurable through proxies, visible in some longitudinal web subdomains, and consequential enough to motivate better crawl curation. Do not convert this into direct evidence for the paper's primary post-training claim. State plainly that the evidence here is partial and indirect: it does not prove web-wide pretraining degradation or contamination. Required counterevidence must be foregrounded, not buried: RefinedWeb and FineWeb show that carefully filtered web data still trains strong models. Use C4 to show that crawl artifacts are real, but not to claim a generalized contemporary failure mode. End with the explicit limitation that no post-2022 web-scale contamination audit exists in the corpus.

anchor_paper.why: It is the best anchor for defining L_auth as a descriptive framework rather than a law: it explicitly compares lexical-diversity and entropy-style complexity indices, shows which are robust to corpus-size and content variation, and therefore supports D2 and D3 as measurable but corpus-sensitive outcome dimensions.

spine.role_in_narrative: Establishes the measurement philosophy for L_auth: lexical diversity and entropy-related indices are useful only when their robustness to corpus size and content is made explicit, which fits D2 and D3 as emergent outcomes rather than intrinsic constants.
spine.transition_to_next: Once diversity metrics are treated as corpus-sensitive rather than absolute, the next step is to clarify how lexical diversity and entropy relate analytically while still tracking different aspects of post-training data composition.

spine.role_in_narrative: Provides the bridge between D2 and D3 by showing a functional relation between type-token behavior and word entropy across corpora, justifying why L_auth keeps them separate but coordinated.
spine.transition_to_next: With D2 and D3 grounded as measurable outcomes, the framework needs evidence that composition shifts in fine-tuning-like data actually move those outcomes in practice.

spine.role_in_narrative: Supplies that applied link: recursive training on synthetic text yields declines in lexical, syntactic, and semantic diversity, making diversity loss a concrete outcome signature of provenance changes.
spine.transition_to_next: That diversity-loss result motivates a broader provenance argument: if synthetic text occupies too much of the training mix, the concern is not only surface variety but contraction of the underlying distribution itself.

spine.role_in_narrative: Generalizes the outcome story into a provenance-ratio story: repeated training on generated data erases tail events, motivating D1 as a design-level input describing how much human-origin versus model-origin data is in the fine-tuning pool.
spine.transition_to_next: Having framed provenance ratio as consequential, the next question is whether these effects are inevitable or whether loop design can moderate them.

spine.role_in_narrative: Introduces the first explicit moderation claim in the spine: under an idealized correction function, self-consuming loops can be stabilized, implying that provenance effects depend on training design rather than expressing a universal law.
spine.transition_to_next: If collapse depends on how recursive data are managed, then curation and socially grounded selection become natural candidate inputs for the framework's design-side dimensions, including D4.

spine.role_in_narrative: Ends the spine by showing that curated self-consuming data can behave differently under strong assumptions, which motivates L_auth's final move: D1 and D4 are design-level composition variables, while D2 and D3 are the measurable outcomes they may influence.
spine.transition_to_next: Together these papers justify L_auth as a synthesis for post-training data composition; they do not validate it as a standalone law, a detector, or a stage-agnostic result, and they leave cross-dimension weight calibration open.

supporting.role: Operationalizes entropy as a composition-sensitive signal by showing that Renyi entropy tracks topic structure and regularization effects; useful support for D3 as a tunable outcome metric rather than a single canonical score.
Extends the entropy branch with renormalization across topic granularities, reinforcing that entropy-style measurements depend on scale and modeling choices.

Genre/source comparison showing that literary and newspaper corpora differ strongly in lexical richness and structural variation, giving a concrete corpus-level example for D2 and a weak proxy for D4's source heterogeneity.
Shows lexical diversity varying by proficiency and native-language group, supporting the idea that social-source differences can matter, though it does not by itself define a stable D4 metric.
Independent high-visibility corroboration that recursive training on generated data erodes distributional tails, strengthening D1's provenance-ratio motivation.
Domain-specific replication in medicine where synthetic contamination wipes out rare but clinically important variability, illustrating why provenance ratio matters most when tail cases matter.

Shows that source-language properties and lexical diversity shape learning behavior, helping bridge from observed diversity changes to model effects and giving one of the few usable cues toward D4's social-behavioral input dimension.
Provides a next-token-distribution characterization of collapse via surplexity; relevant as an alternative D3-style signal, but not used here as a detection tool.
Shows one mitigation route in diffusion models where synthetic data are handled through negative guidance, supporting the claim that recursive-data effects are design-sensitive.
Another intervention family that extrapolates away from self-training degradation, reinforcing that composition policies can matter without implying a universal corrective recipe.
Uses teacher-entropy-aware distillation to preserve diversity, supporting D3 as an outcome that can be shaped by training policy even when provenance is held fixed.

Adds a formal caution that evaluability of generative-model differences from finite samples is itself limited, which supports ending Beat 3 with scope boundaries rather than calibrated causal weights.

paragraph_outline.topic: Define the measurable outcome side of L_auth: D2 lexical diversity and D3 entropy are related but distinct, and both require robustness-aware operationalization.
paragraph_outline.opening_sentence: We define L_auth first on the outcome side: lexical diversity and entropy are not interchangeable quality labels, but corpus-sensitive measurements whose usefulness depends on robust operationalization across domains, sizes, and source populations.

paragraph_outline.topic: Introduce the provenance-sensitive mechanism behind those outcomes: changes in data origin can reduce diversity and compress tails, motivating D1 as a design input while only weakly sketching D4 through source-group variation.
paragraph_outline.opening_sentence: The framework's input side is motivated by evidence that when model-origin data becomes too dominant, measurable outcomes shift: linguistic diversity declines, rare events disappear, and source properties begin to shape what the learner can still represent.

paragraph_outline.topic: State the design-side scope of L_auth: provenance ratio and socially grounded curation appear to matter, but the literature shows conditional moderation rather than a validated law, leaving calibration and generalization open.
paragraph_outline.opening_sentence: What the recursive-training literature currently supports is not a stage-agnostic law of authenticity, but a narrower design claim: outcomes depend on how synthetic and human data are mixed, corrected, curated, and evaluated during post-training.

writing_notes: Keep the prose definitional, not triumphalist. Present L_auth as a fine-tuning-focused descriptive synthesis with four dimensions: D1 Provenance Ratio and D4 Social Behavioral Diversity as design-level inputs; D2 Lexical Diversity and D3 Entropy as measurable emergent outcomes. Be explicit that evidence is strongest for D1->D2/D3 style relationships and much thinner for D4, which is only partially proxied here by genre, proficiency group, and source-language differences. Do not call L_auth a validated law, discovery, detector, or stage-agnostic framework. Say directly that pretraining operationalization remains future work, and that weight calibration across D1-D4 is also future work rather than something established by the cited literature.

beat_name: Fine-tuning Data Source Affects Social Reasoning

anchor_paper.why: Anchor paper for Beat 4: LIMA shows that a small, carefully curated human-authored set can drive large alignment gains, making data provenance a high-leverage post-training variable.

spine.role_in_narrative: Establishes why social reasoning is the right downstream target: even strong LLMs lag on mental-state and norm-sensitive tasks, so this capability cannot be assumed from pretraining alone.
spine.transition_to_next: Once social reasoning is established as a persistent weak spot, the next question is whether carefully curated post-training data can move that behavior more efficiently than scale alone. [thematic progression; no verified internal citation edge]
spine.ordering_note: No direct internal citation edge was found; order is justified by the argument progression rather than by a verified citation chain.

spine.role_in_narrative: Anchor paper for Beat 4: LIMA shows that a small, carefully curated human-authored set can drive large alignment gains, making data provenance a high-leverage post-training variable.
spine.transition_to_next: After LIMA makes curation a plausible treatment variable, the literature asks whether provenance alters socially meaningful outputs rather than only general chat quality. [thematic progression; no verified internal citation edge]
spine.ordering_note: No direct internal citation edge was found; order is justified by the argument progression rather than by a verified citation chain.

spine.role_in_narrative: Shows that post-training data source changes socially meaningful outputs, not just generic helpfulness: instruction tuning shifts whose opinions the model appears to reflect.
spine.transition_to_next: If provenance affects social judgments, then the optimization pipeline itself matters, which is why later work isolates a simpler preference-learning objective. [thematic progression; no verified internal citation edge]
spine.ordering_note: No direct internal citation edge was found; order is justified by the argument progression rather than by a verified citation chain.

spine.role_in_narrative: Provides the clean methodological hinge: DPO simplifies preference optimization so feedback-source comparisons become more interpretable than full RLHF stacks.
spine.transition_to_next: With a simpler alignment method in place, the strongest counterclaim is that AI-generated preference signals may substitute for human judgments on bounded tasks. [thematic progression; no verified internal citation edge]
spine.ordering_note: No direct internal citation edge was found; order is justified by the argument progression rather than by a verified citation chain.

spine.role_in_narrative: Provides required counterevidence: AI feedback can match RLHF on bounded dialogue and summarization tasks, so the claim must stay narrower than universal human-data superiority.

supporting.role: Benchmark anchor showing that social interaction reasoning remains far below human performance.
Human-generated conversation corpus that makes post-training provenance concrete rather than hypothetical.
Low-cost simulated-feedback baseline that narrows any claim of uniquely human preference data.
Extends the baseline diagnosis from benchmark accuracy to pragmatics, showing LLMs still lag human goal-sensitive relevance judgments in question answering.
Adds nuance that some social deficits may reflect failure to externalize latent Theory-of-Mind knowledge rather than complete absence of it.
Shows that human-labeled helpfulness and harmlessness dimensions can be separated and operationalized, reinforcing that socially relevant behavior depends on the annotation source and label design.
Provides complementary evidence that instruction tuning can partially improve Theory of Mind while some reasoning-oriented fine-tuning can worsen false-belief behavior.
Offers synthetic-data counterevidence by showing self-generated instructions can improve instruction following without heavy human annotation.

paragraph_outline.topic: Social reasoning remains a distinct post-training weak spot
paragraph_outline.opening_sentence: The fine-tuning line should begin from task sensitivity, not from collapse theory: social commonsense and theory-of-mind style reasoning remain meaningfully weaker than generic instruction following.

paragraph_outline.topic: Curated human data and provenance shape socially meaningful behavior
paragraph_outline.opening_sentence: Against that backdrop, human conversational corpora and curated instruction sets make data provenance concrete, and LIMA plus opinion-shift evidence show that source quality affects socially meaningful outputs.

paragraph_outline.topic: Methodological hinge plus bounded-task counterevidence
paragraph_outline.opening_sentence: The honest conclusion comes only after the counterexamples: DPO makes source comparisons cleaner, but AlpacaFarm, RLAIF, and Zephyr all show that synthetic or AI-feedback pipelines can succeed on bounded alignment objectives.

writing_notes: Keep Beat 4 clearly separate from collapse literature. The defensible chain is: social reasoning is a real post-training weak spot; curated human data can be disproportionately high leverage; provenance changes socially meaningful outputs; and bounded-task counterevidence prevents any universal claim that human data always wins. The final sentence of the beat should explicitly narrow the thesis to socially grounded, norm-sensitive behavior rather than alignment overall.

anchor_paper.why: Anchor paper for Beat 5: LIMA is the clearest precedent that data quality and curation can matter enough to justify a provenance-sensitive fine-tuning contrast.

spine.role_in_narrative: Establishes Social IQa as a demanding evaluation target, which is why the pilot uses social reasoning rather than only generic chat benchmarks.
spine.transition_to_next: Because the benchmark is intentionally socially grounded, the next step is a precedent showing that carefully curated fine-tuning data can materially change behavior. [thematic progression; no verified internal citation edge]
spine.ordering_note: No direct internal citation edge was found; order is justified by the argument progression rather than by a verified citation chain.

spine.role_in_narrative: Anchor paper for Beat 5: LIMA is the clearest precedent that data quality and curation can matter enough to justify a provenance-sensitive fine-tuning contrast.
spine.transition_to_next: Once data quality is treated as the intervention, the method must stay simple enough that condition-to-condition differences remain interpretable. [thematic progression; no verified internal citation edge]
spine.ordering_note: No direct internal citation edge was found; order is justified by the argument progression rather than by a verified citation chain.

spine.role_in_narrative: Defines the pilot's methodological hinge: DPO keeps the optimization recipe simple enough that differences between data conditions remain interpretable.
spine.transition_to_next: With a controlled preference objective in place, the strongest next baseline is Zephyr, which applies AI-feedback distillation on top of a DPO-style alignment recipe. [verified citation order]
spine.ordering_note: Verified by check_citations.py: Zephyr cites DPO.

spine.role_in_narrative: Provides the strongest competitive synthetic baseline: AI-feedback distillation can produce strong chat alignment, which is why the pilot must be framed as a directional test rather than a foregone human-data victory.

supporting.role: Broader theory-of-mind benchmark evidence showing that social reasoning deficits persist in strong LLMs.
Low-cost simulated-feedback baseline that makes a non-human comparison arm realistic for pilot studies.
Bounded-task counterevidence showing AI feedback can already match RLHF in dialogue and summarization.
Provides evaluation-hygiene guidance for social-behavior studies, reinforcing that benchmark choice, prompt documentation, and contamination checks matter for interpreting any pilot result.
Adds a pragmatics-oriented counterpoint: even if benchmark accuracy moves, human-like goal-sensitive relevance judgments may remain missing.
Extends the synthetic-data story with principle-driven self-alignment, showing another low-human-supervision route for post-training behavior change.
Provides a ranking-loss alternative to DPO, showing that contrastive alignment can be framed without full RL and that the objective family is broader than one algorithm.
Introduces process supervision as a competing training-signal design, underscoring that any observed effect in the pilot may reflect supervision form as much as data source.

paragraph_outline.topic: Why the pilot uses social reasoning as its evaluation target
paragraph_outline.opening_sentence: The experiment beat should begin by justifying the evaluation target: social reasoning remains difficult enough that small changes in data provenance are more likely to surface here than on generic chat benchmarks.

paragraph_outline.topic: Why a provenance-sensitive contrast is methodologically plausible
paragraph_outline.opening_sentence: LIMA motivates the treatment variable, DPO keeps the comparison interpretable, and AlpacaFarm shows why a synthetic baseline is methodologically reasonable even if it is not the same as socially grounded human data.

paragraph_outline.topic: Why the outcome must be framed as directional rather than validated
paragraph_outline.opening_sentence: The strongest existing counterevidence comes from bounded alignment tasks: RLAIF and Zephyr show that AI feedback can already be highly competitive, which is why the present experiment can only offer directional evidence on social reasoning rather than full validation.

writing_notes: Beat 5 must stay method-and-pilot framed. The clearest inspectable chain is benchmark sensitivity -> LIMA as quality-sensitive precedent -> DPO as controlled optimization method -> Zephyr as the strongest AI-feedback baseline, with AlpacaFarm and RLAIF used as supporting narrowing evidence rather than as extra spine detours. End on limitations, not victory language: a 3B model may not separate data conditions sharply, D1 and D4 co-vary in the design, and the strongest non-human baselines come from bounded alignment tasks rather than dedicated social-reasoning comparisons.

anchor_paper.why: It is the closest AI-facing articulation of the exact infrastructure gaps CampusGo is meant to address—authenticity, consent tracking, and provenance—while still allowing an honest framing that CampusGo is a deployed system contribution rather than evidence of downstream model improvement.

spine.role_in_narrative: Provides the broad feasibility precedent: mobile sensing can capture rich human behavioral traces over time.
spine.transition_to_next: A general sensing precedent becomes relevant only after it is narrowed into a campus-specific behavioral setting. [verified citation order]

spine.role_in_narrative: Narrows that feasibility claim into the university setting by showing that continuous smartphone sensing can characterize student behavior in a campus environment.
spine.transition_to_next: Once campus collection looks feasible, the key question becomes not sensing alone but how authenticity, consent, and provenance should be represented for AI use. [thematic progression; no verified internal citation edge]
spine.ordering_note: No direct internal citation edge was found; order is justified by the argument progression rather than by a verified citation chain.

spine.role_in_narrative: Anchor paper for Beat 6: AI data authenticity, consent, and provenance are currently fragmented, so a deployed CampusGo-style collection platform needs these requirements baked in from the start.
spine.ordering_note: No direct internal citation edge was found; order is justified by the argument progression rather than by a verified citation chain.

spine.role_in_narrative: Adds the stewardship boundary: contributors must remain stakeholders in how sensitive data are curated and reused, which keeps CampusGo deployed-but-scoped rather than extractive or overclaimed.
spine.ordering_note: No direct internal citation edge was found; order is justified by the argument progression rather than by a verified citation chain.

spine.role_in_narrative: Structured Beat 6 spine paper.

supporting.role: Power-sensitive governance lens reinforcing that open-data defaults are insufficient for contributor-originated data.
Qualifies interoperability goals by showing that harmonization can trade away information, supporting careful schema design rather than maximal standardization.
Provides a concrete lifecycle-accounting precedent from health data, reinforcing that integrity claims depend on documented provenance, completeness, and contemporaneity checks.

Offers a practical implementation precedent for version control and community governance, useful for arguing that provenance requires maintained change histories rather than one-time metadata capture.
Extends the stewardship argument into AI-adjacent biomedical settings, emphasizing integrity, privacy, and accessibility as ongoing curation tasks.
Acts as a technical provenance-systems analogue for traceable AI pipelines, though it should be treated as supporting systems evidence rather than a settled standard.

paragraph_outline.topic: Campus-situated collection is technically plausible
paragraph_outline.opening_sentence: The beat should start with implementation feasibility only: adjacent mobile-sensing work shows that campus-scale behavioral collection is technically plausible, not that deployment alone proves model-training value.

paragraph_outline.topic: AI-specific provenance requirements define the proposal core
paragraph_outline.opening_sentence: What turns simple sensing into a defensible proposal is the provenance layer plus a downstream use case: authenticity, consent, and governance must be designed together, and OpenAssistant shows that intentionally collected human conversations can in fact become alignment data.

paragraph_outline.topic: Stewardship requirements keep CampusGo deployed but carefully scoped
paragraph_outline.opening_sentence: The final move is a scope limit: stewardship principles show why CampusGo can be presented as a deployed provenance-aware platform, but not as a validated intervention for downstream model gains.

writing_notes: Beat 6 should read as deployed requirements engineering, not downstream validation. Start from campus feasibility, pivot to the AI-specific provenance/authenticity problem, and then use OpenAssistant as the concrete bridge showing that intentionally collected human interactions can become alignment corpora. End on governance and stewardship constraints. Do not claim that any existing paper proves CampusGo will improve downstream models; the literature only motivates what such a deployed system would need to satisfy.

anchor_paper.why: It is the most direct scope-limiting paper for this beat because it shows Theory-of-Mind gains from inference-time scaling alone, without changing model architecture or training data. That makes it the clearest evidence that any thesis here should be narrowed to fixed-compute data-composition effects rather than treated as a general explanation of social-reasoning improvement.

spine.role_in_narrative: Establishes the first serious competing mechanism: test-time compute can raise reasoning quality by aggregating multiple sampled chains, even when training data stay fixed.
spine.transition_to_next: If extra inference-time search already improves reasoning, the next issue is whether more structured reasoning formats outperform plain sampling and therefore further limit any data-only account.

spine.role_in_narrative: Moves from generic self-consistency to structured chain-of-thought, showing that symbolic scaffolding can improve reasoning beyond standard CoT.
spine.transition_to_next: That progression matters for scoping because the real question is not only whether reasoning improves at test time, but whether social reasoning itself can improve the same way.

spine.role_in_narrative: Provides the strongest direct alternative for this beat by demonstrating Theory-of-Mind gains from verifier-guided inference-time scaling alone, including on smaller models.
spine.transition_to_next: Once social-reasoning gains can arise from inference-time compute alone, honest scoping requires widening the comparison set to training-stage and elicitation mechanisms that may also shift behavior.

spine.role_in_narrative: Broadens the alternative set beyond test-time compute by showing that pragmatic competence changes across pre-training, SFT, and preference-optimization stages.
spine.transition_to_next: If competence depends on training stage as well as inference-time procedure, then targeted supervision and reward design become additional nontrivial explanations for observed gains.

spine.role_in_narrative: Closes the main thread with social-reasoning-specific optimization evidence, indicating that process rewards and adversarial training can improve behavior without appealing to the focal data-composition story.
spine.transition_to_next: Taken together, these papers argue for a narrow claim: any contribution here should be framed as a fixed-compute data-composition effect, not as a general account of social-reasoning improvement.

supporting.role: Preprint version of the same symbolic-CoT line; useful as corroboration and chronology, but not a separate conceptual step.
Extends the inference-time/meta-reasoning alternative into a ToM-oriented architecture, reinforcing that deployment-time control can be a real source of gains.
Shows that process supervision can substantially improve reasoning, supporting the broader point that objective design can matter independently of corpus composition.
Adds an optimization-side alternative: distillation objective choices can preserve diversity and alignment, so reasoning behavior need not reduce to data effects.
Argues that models may already contain latent ToM knowledge and mainly need better externalization through steering, which weakens any strong data-only interpretation.
Shows that in-context examples can materially change alignment behavior at deployment time, underscoring that prompt/context composition is another genuine competing mechanism.
Highlights that large-scale curated pretraining data can improve reasoning, reminding the reader to separate fixed-compute composition claims from sheer corpus scale and curation.
Provides a scale/modality boundary: frontier VLMs can look strong on headline ToM metrics while still failing on false-belief probes and showing social desirability bias.
Supplies a pragmatic benchmark where human-oriented probabilistic models still outperform LLMs, preventing overgeneralization from isolated gains.
Preprint version of the same pragmatic-relevance result; useful as a duplicate source trail rather than a distinct argumentative move.

paragraph_outline.topic: Inference-time scaling and structured reasoning are genuine alternatives to data-based explanations.
paragraph_outline.opening_sentence: A first and nontrivial competing explanation is that reasoning improvements can emerge from inference-time computation and structure alone, even with no change to the underlying training corpus.

paragraph_outline.topic: Training-stage, supervision, optimization, and steering mechanisms provide additional explanations for gains in pragmatic or social behavior.
paragraph_outline.opening_sentence: A second family of alternatives shifts attention from test-time compute to how competence is elicited, optimized, or externalized through supervision and training design.

paragraph_outline.topic: Context effects, data scale, and hard pragmatic evaluations all narrow the scope of any broad causal claim.
paragraph_outline.opening_sentence: Finally, deployment context, corpus scale and curation, and tougher pragmatic evaluations all caution against treating one mechanism as a complete explanation of social-reasoning performance.

writing_notes: Write this beat as a concessionary scope paragraph, not as a rebuttal section. State early that inference-time scaling is a real alternative, and use DEL-ToM as the sharpest reason to narrow the claim. Treat the SymbCoT and Relevant answers pairs as duplicate venue/preprint trails rather than independent evidence bursts. End explicitly on the boundary: the contribution, if maintained, is about data-composition effects under fixed model size and test-time budget, not about universally explaining or maximizing social reasoning.
